title: Exposure Tracking and Organ Radiosensitivity
narrationScript: Your dose record is the one document that follows you for the rest of your working life. This lesson covers how the industry tracks the radiation a person accumulates over a career, and why that matters. Start with tracking. Each monitoring period your badge is processed, and that result becomes your dose of record. Those numbers do not reset at year end. They add, into a cumulative occupational dose kept for life. When you change employers, the new one requests your prior dose so the count continues unbroken, because the limit is yours, not the job's. Now why it matters. Radiation does not treat all tissue alike. The Bergonie and Tribondeau law says cells are most sensitive when they divide fast, are immature, and have a long dividing future. So bone marrow, reproductive cells, the intestinal lining, and the lens of the eye are most radiosensitive. Skin and most organs sit in the middle. Muscle, bone, and mature nerve are least sensitive. That ranking is exactly why the whole-body limit is ten times stricter than the skin limit.

sections.heading: Your Dose Record Is for Life
sections.body:
Personnel monitoring does two jobs. Day to day, it tells you whether your technique is keeping dose low. Over a career, it builds the permanent legal record of every bit of occupational radiation you have received — and that second job is the subject of this lesson.

The key idea is that **occupational dose accumulates and is never erased**. Each monitoring period, your badge — film, TLD, or OSL — is processed by an accredited lab, and the result becomes your **dose of record**: the official number entered in your file for that period. The annual limit (5 rem whole-body under the typical NRC / Agreement-State model) governs each year, but the individual readings do not vanish when a year closes. They are summed into a **cumulative occupational dose** — a running lifetime total that follows the *person*, not the employer or the job.

That distinction matters enormously. Because the limit attaches to you, no employer can hand you a 'fresh' allowance, and the count cannot be restarted by switching companies. Your record is a ledger that only ever grows.

sections.heading: Prior Dose: Closing the Gaps
sections.body:
If dose follows the worker, the tracking system has to survive a change of employer — otherwise every job change would quietly reset the count. It does not, and the mechanism is the **prior-dose inquiry**.

Before an employer lets you take on planned occupational dose, they are expected to make a reasonable effort to obtain your dose history from **previous employers** for the current year, so that your annual total reflects radiation received *everywhere*, not just at the current site. In the NRC system this runs on standardized paperwork — a **Form 4** style record carries your dose for the current monitoring year, and a **Form 5** style record is the cumulative occupational dose maintained for the life of the worker.

The practical consequences for a working radiographer:

- Expect to **disclose prior radiation work** when you are hired, and expect your new employer to request those records before assigning you to dose-bearing work.
- A heavy dose earned at one company **carries forward** — it does not stay behind when you move on.
- The records are kept **essentially forever** and are among the first documents a regulator or an investigator reviews.

This is why an honest, complete record at every employer protects not just you, but the next crew you join.

sections.caption: How a career dose is tracked: a prior-dose inquiry pulls history from past employers, each processed badge becomes the dose of record, and every period adds to a lifetime cumulative ledger that never resets.

sections.title: Dose of record vs. pocket dosimeter
sections.body: Two numbers, two purposes. Your **pocket dosimeter** gives an immediate, on-the-job reading you use to manage dose in real time — but it is not your legal dose. Your **dose of record** is the processed result from your film, TLD, or OSL badge, established by an accredited lab; that is the number entered in your lifetime file and summed into your cumulative total. When the two disagree, the **badge** is the record of legal account, and the discrepancy gets investigated.

sections.heading: Why Tissue Sensitivity Drives Everything
sections.body:
Tracking dose only matters because radiation harms tissue — and the second half of this lesson explains *why a given dose matters more to some tissues than to others*. That is the concept of **radiosensitivity**: the relative susceptibility of a cell, tissue, or organ to radiation damage.

The governing principle is the **law of Bergonié and Tribondeau**: cells are generally **more radiosensitive** when they are

- **rapidly dividing** (high mitotic rate),
- **undifferentiated** or immature (not yet specialized for a final job), and
- have a **long dividing future** ahead of them.

The logic is mechanical. Radiation does its lasting damage to DNA, and a cell is most vulnerable to a DNA error when it is about to copy that DNA and divide. A cell that divides constantly has many of those vulnerable moments; a cell that essentially never divides has almost none. The same rule explains why a developing embryo or fetus — nothing but rapidly dividing, undifferentiated cells — is far more radiosensitive than the adult who carries it, and why the declared-pregnancy limit is so much lower.

sections.heading: Ranking the Tissues
sections.body:
Apply the Bergonié–Tribondeau rule to the body and a clear ranking falls out, from most to least radiosensitive.

**Highly sensitive** tissues are the body's cell factories. **Bone marrow and the lymphocytes** it produces are among the most radiosensitive tissue of all — which is why a falling blood count is the earliest measurable sign of a serious whole-body exposure. The **reproductive (gonadal) cells**, the rapidly renewing **lining of the small intestine**, and the **lens of the eye** round out the high-sensitivity group.

**Intermediately sensitive** tissues divide, but more slowly: **skin** and other surface linings, and the bulk organ tissue of the **lung, liver, and kidney**.

**Least sensitive** tissues are mature and rarely divide: **muscle and bone**, and fully developed **nerve cells**. Note the reversal with age — bone is radiosensitive in a growing child but resistant in an adult, exactly as the 'long dividing future' clause predicts.

Keep the headline straight: **fast-dividing, immature tissue is sensitive; mature, static tissue is resistant.**

sections.caption: Tissues ranked by relative radiosensitivity into high, intermediate, and low bands — the same ranking that sets tissue weighting factors and explains why the whole-body limit is far stricter than the skin limit.
Relative radiosensitivity of body tissues, organized by the Bergonié–Tribondeau law.
rows: Bone marrow and lymphocytes, gonads (reproductive cells), intestinal lining, lens of the eye, embryo/fetus
Rapidly dividing, undifferentiated, long dividing future
Skin and surface epithelium; lung, liver, kidney tissue
Muscle, bone (in adults), mature nerve cells
Specialized and rarely or never dividing

sections.title: From radiosensitivity to dose limits
sections.body: Radiosensitivity is not just biology trivia — it is wired into how dose is regulated. Dose-calculation systems apply **tissue weighting factors**: a unit of dose to a highly radiosensitive tissue is counted as contributing more whole-body risk than the same dose to a resistant one. The same ranking is why the annual **skin and extremity limit (50 rem)** is ten times the **whole-body limit (5 rem)** — skin tolerates far more dose than the radiosensitive blood-forming and reproductive tissue the whole-body limit is built to protect.

figures.title: Tracking cumulative occupational dose over a career

interactive.title: Rank the Tissue
interactive.intro: The chart sorts tissues into high, intermediate, and low radiosensitivity. Use the Bergonié–Tribondeau rule — fast-dividing, immature, long dividing future means more sensitive — to answer the question. Click the band that contains the body's most radiosensitive tissues.
interactive.prompt: Which band holds the tissues that are damaged first and at the lowest dose — the ones the whole-body limit is written to protect?

regions.label: High-sensitivity band
regions.feedback: Correct. Bone marrow and lymphocytes, gonads, intestinal lining, and the lens of the eye are the most radiosensitive tissues — rapidly dividing and undifferentiated — which is why a falling blood count is the first measurable sign of a serious exposure.

regions.label: Intermediate-sensitivity band
regions.feedback: Not the most sensitive. Skin and the bulk organ tissue of the lung, liver, and kidney divide at a moderate rate, placing them in the middle of the ranking — more resistant than marrow and gonads.

regions.label: Low-sensitivity band
regions.feedback: These are the most resistant tissues, not the most sensitive. Muscle, adult bone, and mature nerve cells rarely divide, so by the Bergonié–Tribondeau law they tolerate the most dose.

checkQuestions.question: What is a worker's 'cumulative occupational dose'?
options: The running lifetime total of occupational dose, summed across all periods and all employers and never reset
The highest single dose recorded in any one monitoring period
The dose remaining before the annual 5 rem limit is reached
The average yearly dose over the worker's career
checkQuestions.explanation: Each period's dose of record adds to a permanent lifetime ledger that follows the worker across jobs. It does not reset annually or when changing employers — the annual limit governs each year, but the cumulative total only grows.

checkQuestions.question: Before assigning a newly hired radiographer to dose-bearing work, an employer is expected to:
options: Issue a fresh annual allowance, since dose limits reset with each new employer
Make a reasonable effort to obtain the worker's dose history from previous employers for the current year
Wait until the worker has completed one full year before requesting any records
Use only the worker's verbal estimate of past dose
checkQuestions.explanation: The prior-dose inquiry keeps the annual total accurate across job changes. Because the limit follows the person, the new employer must account for dose received elsewhere in the same year — in the NRC system this rides on Form 4 / Form 5 style records.

checkQuestions.question: According to the law of Bergonié and Tribondeau, cells are generally MORE radiosensitive when they are:
options: Mature, specialized, and rarely dividing
Rapidly dividing, undifferentiated, and have a long dividing future
Located near the surface of the body
Large in size and rich in water
checkQuestions.explanation: Bergonié–Tribondeau ties radiosensitivity to mitotic activity, lack of differentiation, and dividing future. Radiation damages DNA, and a cell is most vulnerable when it is about to copy that DNA and divide.

checkQuestions.question: Which tissue is among the MOST radiosensitive in the body?
options: Mature nerve cells
Muscle tissue
Cartilage
Bone-marrow blood-forming cells and lymphocytes
checkQuestions.explanation: The blood-forming bone marrow and the lymphocytes it produces divide constantly and are highly radiosensitive — a falling blood count is the earliest measurable sign of a serious whole-body exposure. Mature nerve, muscle, and cartilage rarely divide and are radioresistant.

checkQuestions.question: How does tissue radiosensitivity relate to the dose limits a radiographer works under?
options: It has no connection — dose limits are set the same for every tissue
The most radiosensitive tissues are given the highest dose limits
Radiosensitivity sets tissue weighting factors and explains why the whole-body limit (5 rem) is far stricter than the skin/extremity limit (50 rem)
Only skin sensitivity is considered, because skin receives the dose first
checkQuestions.explanation: Weighting factors count dose to radiosensitive tissue as more harmful, and the limits follow the same logic: skin tolerates far more (50 rem) than the radiosensitive blood-forming and reproductive tissue the 5 rem whole-body limit protects.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024 Radiation Safety Outline §8.7–8.8 (personnel monitoring for tracking exposure; organ radiosensitivity)
Typical NRC 10 CFR 20-style requirements for cumulative occupational dose records and prior-dose determination (Forms 4 and 5), described generically — your license and procedures govern
Law of Bergonié and Tribondeau and standard tissue weighting factors (ICRP) for relative radiosensitivity
